common_name: Pasilla Negro Pepper (Mexican Mole Chile, Long Dark-Fruited Dried Chile)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Pasilla Negro is typically harvested at the dark chocolate-brown to very dark green stage (not fully red — though the fruits do ripen through dark brown to red). In zone 9, transplant March–April. Allow some fruits to dry completely on the plant or harvested and air-dry for storage as the dried pasilla chile used in mole negro, enchilada sauces, and other traditional Mexican preparations. Fresh pasilla is used as a stuffed pepper (chile relleno) and in sauces.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 78–85°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. Pasilla Negro (also called Chile Pasilla, Chile Negro, or Pasilla Bajío) is a long, narrow, dark-fruited Mexican chile pepper that, when dried, becomes one of the essential dried chiles of Mexican cuisine alongside ancho and mulato. The fresh pepper is 6–9 inches long, narrow (1–1.5 inches diameter), and deep dark green to near-black at the harvest stage — the name 'Pasilla' (little raisin) refers to the wrinkled, dark, raisin-like appearance of the dried pepper. The heat level is mild to medium (1,000–2,500 Scoville units) — substantially milder than serrano or jalapeño — making the peppers appropriate for use in larger quantities in sauces and moles. The flavor is rich, earthy, and slightly fruity with a complex character that makes pasilla one of the most important 'flavor' peppers in Mexican cooking rather than primarily a heat source. Mole negro, the complex black mole of Oaxaca and southern Mexico, typically uses pasilla negro (along with mulato and ancho) as its primary chile base, and the dried, toasted pasilla contributes the dark color and deep, earthy flavor that defines the sauce.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Pasilla Negro chile variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Mexican dried chile collection
University of New Mexico — Chile Pepper Institute: Pasilla Negro documentation

common_name: Golden Ball Turnip (Yellow-Fleshed Sweet Heirloom Fall Turnip)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for the best turnip production in zone 9; turnips grown in cool fall weather are sweeter and less pungent than summer-grown turnips. In zone 9, sow August–October for harvest October–January. Spring planting is possible (February–March) but quality is often reduced by warming temperatures. Golden Ball's yellow flesh is notably sweeter than standard purple-top white-fleshed types and makes it excellent roasted.

directions: Direct sow 0.25 inch deep in rows 12 inches apart; thin to 4–6 inches as plants establish. Golden Ball (also known as Robertson's Golden Ball and Amber Globe) is a Victorian-era heirloom turnip with a strikingly different appearance from the standard purple-top white-fleshed types that dominate American turnip culture: the round, smooth roots are golden-yellow skinned with deep golden-yellow flesh, rather than the white flesh of standard types. The yellow flesh color indicates a different carotenoid profile and, notably, a distinct flavor: Golden Ball is considered one of the sweetest, mildest turnips available — the yellow-fleshed types tend toward greater sweetness and less sulfur-pungency than white-fleshed types when grown in the same conditions. This mild, sweet quality makes Golden Ball excellent for eating raw (rare for turnips), roasted whole (the golden color makes an attractive presentation), and in preparations where the sulfur bite of standard turnips would be unwelcome. Golden Ball has been grown in British kitchen gardens since at least the 1850s and represents the traditional European approach to turnip cultivation that emphasized flavor diversity across multiple colors and flesh types rather than the American focus on the single purple-top type.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Golden Ball turnip variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Heirloom turnip collection
Royal Horticultural Society — Traditional British vegetable variety documentation

common_name: Dragon Tongue Mustard (Colorful Streaked Chinese Mustard Green)

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for best production in zone 9; Dragon Tongue Mustard is a cool-season green that performs best when temperatures are below 75°F. In zone 9, sow August–November for fall and winter harvest, or February–March for spring. Harvest young leaves at 30–40 days for mildest flavor, or allow plants to reach full size (45+ days) for more pungent, mature leaves. The distinctive red-purple veining on bright green leaves intensifies in cool weather.

directions: Direct sow seeds 0.25 inch deep in rows 12 inches apart; thin to 4–6 inches. Harvest outer leaves from 30 days or cut the whole plant at 40–45 days. Dragon Tongue Mustard is a visually striking Asian mustard green with broad, frilly, bright green leaves overlaid with vivid red-purple veining and midribs — the colorful contrasting veination giving the leaves a dramatic appearance unlike any other mustard type and making Dragon Tongue one of the most ornamental edible greens in the winter garden. The name describes the leaf form: long, broad, and pointed like a tongue, with the red-purple coloring suggesting the fieriness appropriate to a dragon. The flavor is spicy, sharp, and pungent in the characteristic Asian mustard style — more aggressive than mild Asian greens like tatsoi or pak choi, but that pungency mellows significantly with cooking. Raw leaves are assertive and best used in small quantities as a spicy accent in salad mixes; cooked leaves (sautéed, steamed, or braised) are excellent alongside other winter vegetables and complement pork, sausage, and beans. Dragon Tongue grows rapidly in cool weather and is one of the most productive and visually interesting additions to a fall and winter garden in the Gulf South.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Mustard green variety comparison
Kitazawa Seed Company — Asian mustard green varieties
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Asian Greens for Southern Gardens

common_name: Long Black Spanish Radish (Winter Storage Radish with Black Skin)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer/early fall for fall and winter harvest; Long Black Spanish is a winter radish that requires a longer growing time than spring radishes (60–70 vs. 25–35 days). In zone 9, sow August–September for November–January harvest. Winter radishes should not be planted in spring — they bolt quickly in warming temperatures. Harvest when roots reach 6–8 inches; they keep in the ground for weeks and can be stored in damp sand in a cool location for months.

directions: Direct sow 0.5 inch deep in fall in rows 12 inches apart; thin to 4–6 inches. Harvest when roots are 6–8 inches long; peel before eating as the black skin is tough and astringent. Long Black Spanish Radish is a winter storage radish with a very distinctive appearance: the long (6–10 inch), cylindrical root has deeply black skin — nearly jet black — contrasting with crisp, white flesh within. It is one of the oldest documented European radish varieties, with references dating to the 16th century; it was the standard large winter radish of European cuisine before the introduction of Asian types and remains common in Polish, German, and Ashkenazi Jewish food traditions where it appears in salads (grated with onion and oil) and as a medicinal digestive herb. The flavor is significantly sharper and more pungent than spring radishes — a biting, horseradish-adjacent pungency that mellows somewhat when the grated flesh is salted and rested. The winter storage capability distinguishes Long Black Spanish from spring radishes: properly harvested and stored in damp conditions near freezing, the roots keep for 3–4 months, providing a root vegetable throughout the winter.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Long Black Spanish radish variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Winter radish collection
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Root Cellar Storage Vegetable Guide

common_name: Munich Beer Radish (Münchener Bier Traditional German Oktoberfest Radish)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer or early fall; Munich Beer Radish is a traditional fall/winter radish larger than spring types. In zone 9, sow August–September for October–December harvest — the timing matches the September–October Oktoberfest season for which this radish is traditional. Harvest when roots are 3–4 inches in diameter and still firm; they can be left in the ground for 2–3 weeks after reaching full size without quality loss. Peel and score in a spiral pattern for the traditional 'radish blossom' Oktoberfest presentation.

directions: Direct sow 0.5 inch deep in early fall in rows 12 inches apart; thin to 4–6 inches for full-sized roots. Harvest when 3–4 inches in diameter. Munich Beer Radish (Münchener Bier Radish, München Bier Radish) is a traditional German fall radish with a cultural identity unlike any other radish variety — it is the specific radish served at Munich's Oktoberfest beer festival, where it appears as 'Radi' (the Bavarian term for radish), peeled in a spiral and salted to create a crunchy, salty accompaniment to the beer. The spiral-peeling technique (using a special peeling tool to create a continuous thin spiral of the white flesh, then pressing it into a salt dish) is a specific Oktoberfest tradition performed at tableside in the festival tents and at traditional Bavarian beer gardens. Munich Beer Radish produces large (3–5 inch), round to slightly elongated, pale rose-skinned roots with white flesh — milder than spring radishes, crisper and slightly sweeter than the pungent Black Spanish types, with a pleasant, refreshing quality appropriate to accompaniment with beer. The round form of the roots lends itself well to the spiral peeling tradition; longer varieties would not work as effectively. Munich Beer Radish is a distinctive specialty item for gardeners interested in brewing culture, German garden traditions, or simply a mild, attractive large fall radish.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Münchener Bier radish variety notes
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — European heirloom radish collection
Traditional Bavarian food culture documentation — Radi at Oktoberfest
